analysis:
- Almost all high‑scoring designs share a common pipeline: deterministic Pareto prefilter → compact O(k)-proportional shortlist scored by a cheap proxy (box-volume ± multi‑weight scalarization) → CELF-style lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs → bounded 1‑for‑1 local swaps → deterministic padding. That combination minimizes expensive pg.hypervolume calls while keeping selection quality high.
- Strengths in the top heuristics: careful shortlist sizing tied to k/pareto size, CELF timestamping to avoid recomputing stale gains, singleton and selective pairwise HV caches, deterministic tie‑breaking, and final swap passes that only evaluate promising exchanges.
- Costly or weaker patterns to avoid: repeated full vstack/array reallocation inside inner loops, frequent Python-level membership checks on lists (use sets), brute‑force recomputation of HV for every candidate without lazy updates, large unbounded shortlists that explode HV calls, and excessive randomness that harms reproducibility.
- Useful pruning/efficiency levers seen across versions: box‑volume or singleton HV upper bounds to skip candidates; limiting pairwise caching to small shortlists; explicit HV‑call budgets and fallbacks (box proxy) when budget is hit; precomputing maps and using vectorized nondominated filtering.
- Algorithmic tradeoffs evident: larger shortlists + more HV calls raise quality but cost; denser scalar probes improve HVC approximation but add CPU/memory; CELF + cached HVs gives large HV savings but must be paired with good shortlist ordering to be effective.
- Implementation nitpicks that repeatedly appear: inconsistent reference‑point handling, redundant type/shape checks inside hot loops, and using list membership for speed‑critical paths. Small fixes (use sets, preallocate buffers, avoid repeated pg.hypervolume object creation) yield big runtime wins.
experience: Design candidates by (1) Pareto prefilter + deterministic blended box+scalar shortlist sized O(k); (2) CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and small selective pairwise cache; (3) tight box/HV upper‑bound pruning and an HV‑call budget with box‑fallback estimates; (4) incremental or cached local‑swap evaluations; and (5) vectorized filters and set membership to cut Python overhead. Tune shortlist size and weight probes for the budget/instance.
current_reflection: Design candidates by (1) Pareto prefilter + deterministic blended box+scalar shortlist sized O(k); (2) CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and small selective pairwise cache; (3) tight box/HV upper‑bound pruning and an HV‑call budget with box‑fallback estimates; (4) incremental or cached local‑swap evaluations; and (5) vectorized filters and set membership to cut Python overhead. Tune shortlist size and weight probes for the budget/instance.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Favor deterministic shortlists, cheap vectorized proxies, cached singleton (and tiny pairwise) HVs, and CELF lazy greedy with tight upper‑bound pruning and an explicit HV‑call budget. Add a small, cached incremental swap pass. Prioritize data reuse (precompute diffs/scores), avoid Python-level remove/linear searches in hot loops, and keep shortlist sizes O(k) to maximize HV value per expensive call.

Favor small, Pareto-aware shortlists and CELF lazy-greedy with cached singleton HVs. Use vectorized box/scalar proxies to rank candidates, selectively cache pairwise HVs only when list ≤ ~40, enforce an HV-call budget, and prune using cheap upper bounds. Preallocate buffers and minimize vstack/copy inside hot loops; prefer deterministic seeds and tie-breakers. These choices give best hypervolume per HV call.

Favor a hybrid score (box volume × multi‑weight scalarization), build an adaptive shortlist (k‑ and pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs plus small pairwise tables, use CELF lazy greedy with box upper‑bound pruning, cap HV calls, then run a small bounded deterministic swap (incremental HV updates). Vectorize dominance/scoring, preallocate buffers, and avoid expensive Python list operations for membership to get better quality/runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto prefilter, O(k) shortlist, CELF lazy greedy, singleton & small pairwise HV cache, box/HV upper bounds, HV-call budget, incremental swaps, vectorized filters, deterministic seeds.
- Advice: Build a deterministic O(k) shortlist using Pareto + cheap box/scalar proxies; cache singleton HVs and use pairwise caches only for very small lists; run CELF with box-bound pruning and an explicit HV-call budget (fallback to box estimates); finish with a tiny incremental swap using cached deltas.
- Avoid: Large shortlists, repeated Python-level loops/copies, nested HV recomputations, unbounded pairwise caching, and non‑deterministic tie‑breakers.
- Explanation: Tight proxies, caching, CELF, and bounded HV calls maximize hypervolume quality per expensive HV evaluation.
